chapters.title: Serving Current Customers Can Create a Trap

summaryBullets: Seeing how listening only to current best customers can blind a firm to new markets creates clearer judgment.
Good management can still create vulnerability when incentives follow the current customer too tightly.
This matters because strong incumbents often miss disruption because their current customers are not asking for it.
A common mistake is assuming current customer demand reveals the whole future market.
The pattern becomes easier to see when the new offer looks uninteresting to core customers while opening elsewhere.
A practical starting move is to ask who is not being served yet, track low end or new market demand, and separate today's best customer from tomorrow's growth.
keyTakeaways: Seeing how listening only to current best customers can blind a firm to new markets creates clearer judgment. This perspective helps people make clearer decisions and follow through with more discipline when stakes rise.
practice: ask who is not being served yet
track low end or new market demand
separate today's best customer from tomorrow's growth
protect some attention for weak early signals

New ventures need different incentives, sizes, and expectations to learn properly.

The pattern becomes easier to see when the innovation keeps failing mainly because it is being judged too early by legacy rules.
A practical starting move is to create a separate structure when necessary, set metrics that fit the new path, and protect the learning period.
